topics: Making natural colours from plants and earth
Surfaces and materials used by artists
subject: Arts

sections.heading: 🌿 The Nature Quest — Seeing the World Like an Artist
sections.body: Young wizard, your first quest is to truly SEE the world around you — not just glance at it! Artists look at tiny details that most people miss. When you go outside, notice the rough bark of a tree, the smooth pebble on the ground, or the pattern on a leaf — just like how you notice every detail when you are playing cricket or decorating for Diwali. Carry a drawing book and sketch what you discover — shapes, textures, colours, and movement of birds or insects.
keyPoints: Look at nature with full attention — sky, soil, leaves, animals, and textures
Sketch what you see in a visual journal every day to build your artist's power
Every detail matters — rough, smooth, bumpy, or grainy all tell a story

sections.heading: 🌸 The Colour Spell — Making Colours from Nature
sections.body: Here is a magical spell — you can make your own colours from things around you! Long before colour boxes were sold in shops, artists used turmeric for yellow, flowers for pink, charcoal for black, and special stones like lapis lazuli for blue. Think about how turmeric stains your fingers yellow when your mother makes dal at home — that is natural colour! Fruits, vegetables, roots, soil, and minerals can all give us beautiful colours.
keyPoints: Natural colours come from plants like turmeric, flowers, and roots, and from earth like soil, minerals, and charcoal
To make paint, the colour source is powdered finely and mixed with a natural binder like gum from a babul tree, egg, or milk so it sticks to surfaces
Artists have used these natural colours for thousands of years

sections.heading: 🎨 Tints and Shades — The Power of Changing Colours
sections.body: Did you know a single colour can have many moods, just like you feel excited at a cricket match but calm at bedtime? In art, we can make a colour lighter or darker. When you add white to a colour, it becomes a TINT — like how a bright red becomes a soft pink. When you add black to a colour, it becomes a SHADE — like turning sky blue into a deep navy. Think of how the sky changes colour from morning to sunset — that is nature showing you tints and shades!
keyPoints: Colour + White = Tint (lighter, softer version of the colour)
Colour + Black = Shade (darker, deeper version of the colour)
Tints and shades help create mood and feeling in an artwork

sections.heading: 🪨 Ancient Surfaces — Where Artists Painted Before Paper
sections.body: Long before notebooks and sketchbooks existed, brave artist-wizards painted on rocks, cave walls, palm leaves, cloth, and even mud plaster! You can see this in the Bhimbetka cave paintings or the beautiful Ajanta Caves in India. Kalamkari artists painted on cloth using natural dyes, and Kolam patterns are drawn on floors using rice flour. Each surface needed different tools and skills — just like how you play differently on a cricket pitch versus a badminton court.
keyPoints: Ancient artists used surfaces like rock, cave walls, palm leaves, cloth, and mud plaster
Natural dyes, earth colours, and mineral colours were used on these surfaces
Famous examples from India include Bhimbetka rock art, Ajanta cave paintings, Kalamkari, and Kolam

sections.heading: 🌟 Your Own Realm — Create Art Your Way
sections.body: Now you have all the powers of a true art wizard! You have observed nature, made your own colours, learned about tints and shades, and discovered ancient surfaces. The most important spell of all is this — YOUR art comes from YOUR eyes. It does not have to be a big mountain or ocean. It could be the mango tree in your courtyard, a festival rangoli, or a butterfly you saw. Choose your own materials, follow your own imagination, and create something the world has never seen before!
keyPoints: Every artist has their own unique way of seeing and creating — and so do you
Nature all around you — even in your own street or home — can inspire your artwork
Use your observations, your imagination, and your own choice of materials to make art truly yours

summary: In this chapter, young art wizards embark on a powerful quest to see the world through an artist's eyes. You learn to observe nature closely — textures of leaves, colours of soil, shapes of clouds — and record these in drawings. You discover the ancient magic of making your own colours from turmeric, flowers, charcoal, and minerals, just like artists did thousands of years ago in India. You master the skill of creating colour tints by adding white and shades by adding black, understanding how colours change moods just like the sky during sunrise. You also explore the incredible surfaces ancient Indian artists used — from Bhimbetka rocks to Ajanta cave walls to Kalamkari cloth. Finally, you are reminded that the greatest power belongs to you — to use your own eyes, your own imagination, and your own experience to create art that is completely unique to you!
